notes:
Opening opportunity notes for plaintiff, arb-ex05-20260629234720.

Opportunity: `openings:plaintiff`; final filing action allowed: `record_opening_statement`; evidence submission not allowed; evidence reading allowed. Deadline and attempts reviewed.

Record/evidence inspection: `get_case` showed an empty record: no evidence, exhibits, openings, arguments, submitted evidence, or technical reports. `list_evidence` returned an empty list; no visible case-packet files. `case_status` confirmed active opening opportunity and visible evidence count 0. No `stat_evidence`/`read_evidence_range` calls were possible/needed because there is no visible evidence.

Issue breakdown: proposition turns on whether, within Nov. 3, 2025-Jan. 31, 2026 11:59 PM ET, the U.S. commenced a military offensive intended to establish control over any portion of Venezuela. Decisive elements: date window; military offensive; intent to establish control; territory treated as Venezuelan/U.S. under Sept. 6 benchmark; credible-source consensus. Plaintiff theory likely relies on official U.S. military action in Caracas/capture of Maduro, wider military deployment/interdictions, and statements or consequences showing asserted control. Defense theory likely: targeted extraction/law-enforcement action, no occupation, no sustained territorial control, remaining Venezuelan authorities, and official denials that it was an invasion.

Native investigation performed: searched web for U.S. invasion/intervention Venezuela Jan. 2026, State/Defense official pages, and credible source consensus. Leads found but not admitted: official War/Defense article dated Jan. 3, 2026 describing capture of Maduro after U.S. military extraction in Caracas and operation in fortified site; Lead IG Operation Southern Spear PDF describing OSS, U.S. deployments into SOUTHCOM area, Jan. 2 capture from fortified compound in Caracas, transport to USS Iwo Jima/U.S., and tanker interdictions; Brookings analysis identifying adverse Rubio statement that it was not an invasion/no occupation and discussing competing claims about U.S. running Venezuela, lack of large ground force, and international law criticism. State.gov interview pages opened only as technical-difficulty pages; need later retrieval from archives or network transcripts.

Later evidence plan: when evidence admission opens, submit official War/Defense article, Lead IG PDF, official or original media transcript for Rubio denial, White House/press transcript or video for any 'run Venezuela'/'presence' language, and Reuters/AP/BBC/CNN/NPR reporting to establish credible-source consensus and adverse/limiting facts. Need verify source chain, timestamps, and whether any later corrections narrow the event to extraction rather than territorial control.

Self-audit: record proof is currently absent, so opening must be forward-looking and should not cite outside sources as record proof. It should tell the council the real dispute, identify proof that would satisfy or defeat the burden, acknowledge missing record evidence, and preserve plaintiff's theory that a military seizure of a head of state on Venezuelan soil coupled with control-intent evidence can satisfy the market definition even without classic long-term occupation.

Defendant opening work notes, 2026-06-29T23:52Z. Opportunity openings:defendant, action allowed record_opening_statement only; evidence admission unavailable. Inspected get_case and list_evidence: current record contains plaintiff opening only; no exhibits, submitted evidence, technical reports, arguments, or case-packet evidence visible. Evidence list is empty, so no stat/read performed. Proposition requires proof that U.S. invaded Venezuela between 2025-11-03 and 2026-01-31 by commencing a military offensive intended to establish control over any portion of Venezuela; standard preponderance and credible-source consensus. Issue breakdown: plaintiff must prove more than use of force, extraction, arrest, interdiction, sanctions, covert action, or regime pressure; even a small/temporary area can count only if the proof shows intent to establish control over a portion of sovereign territory. Source map/search: broad web search found likely future source targets: official War.gov/Defense Jan. 3, 2026 article re Maduro capture/extraction in Caracas; Lead IG Operation Southern Spear PDF Jan.1-Mar.31 2026 with OSS overview and Maduro capture lead; Brookings/CFR expert summaries; PBS, Chatham House, Guardian, Wikipedia as secondary leads. These are unadmitted and not to be cited as record proof in opening. Evidence assessment: present record proves none of the alleged events; outside leads may be adverse on U.S. armed action in Caracas, so defense should not overdeny. Defense theory should focus on burden and missing link: territorial-control intent, duration, command/admin control, local governmental continuity, consent/cooperation, and credible-source consensus. Later evidence phase should target full official statements/transcripts/video, IG report, operational orders/after-action public summaries, congressional/War Powers materials, and strongest adverse expert/legal reports; verify whether any official denial/correction/consent evidence exists. Journal updated at /aar-codex/aar-work-notes.md.

Merits argument turn notes for plaintiff.

Opportunity: `arguments:plaintiff`; final filing action `submit_argument`; evidence submission allowed; text limit 6000; exhibit limit 9. Refreshed record/evidence with `get_case`, `list_evidence`, and `case_status`: record had plaintiff and defendant openings only; visible evidence was empty before submissions.

Source map: decisive elements are date-window U.S. military offensive, Venezuelan territory, intended control over any portion, and credible-source consensus. Strongest plaintiff sources: official War/Defense article, official Lead IG Operation Southern Spear report, transcript of Jan. 4 ABC interview/reporting, and Brookings expert analysis for consensus/adverse characterization. Strongest defense/adverse sources: Rubio denial that it was an invasion/occupation; characterization as law-enforcement/arrest; no visible U.S. presence per AP; no large-scale ground force.

Retrieval/capture: shell curl attempts for War/Defense page and official PDF returned HTTP 403. OpenClaw web_fetch successfully captured the War article, Brookings analysis, and ABC transcript. Web/PDF viewer exposed relevant Lead IG PDF text; raw PDF download remained blocked. Search also located Reuters/AP/RollCall leads but no time to capture all; ABC transcript carried direct Trump/Rubio/Caine/Raddatz content and adverse AP observation.

Evidence submitted and accepted:
1. `ev_beef2fca851d_submitted-evidence-01-plaintiff-beef2fca851d`, sha256 `beef2fca851d7d5ec0d5fde51bd1ba61271346a6d3ba6aae9fe9e42e41a356b4`: official War/Defense article. Proves official admission of U.S. armed operation in Caracas, fortified military fortress, Maduro capture, USS Iwo Jima custody, 150 aircraft cover for ground extraction force, Maduro out of power, U.S. oversight/running language, and Rodriguez cooperation language. Does not by itself prove permanent occupation or exact operational perimeter.
2. `ev_90b0943b9140_submitted-evidence-02-plaintiff-90b0943b9140`, sha256 `90b0943b91402969c2d3c2f44e609085c8601f3ca38ce634a70588cf910fc36a`: Lead IG Operation Southern Spear extract. Proves official oversight context: OSS initiated Nov. 2025, significant Navy/Marine deployments, strikes/interdictions, tanker seizures, and Jan. 2 capture of Maduro from fortified compound in Caracas, transported to USS Iwo Jima and U.S. Limitations: classified mission statement, raw PDF blocked.
3. `ev_51105c5e227a_submitted-evidence-03-plaintiff-51105c5e227a`, sha256 `51105c5e227a4b40fd0e5419f9ae524bfd0be5514277be08c57321c21b8e7d1f`: Brookings analysis. Secondary but useful for credible-source characterization and adverse facts: Rubio denial/no occupation, Trump run Venezuela claims, no large ground force, legal criticism.
4. `ev_9a260d070386_submitted-evidence-04-plaintiff-9a260d070386`, sha256 `9a260d07038642e36241d2f5f68b36a23a7ae276dacef85698a4f00bbdeddd06`: ABC This Week transcript. Proves direct transcript/reporting: U.S. military apprehension in Caracas, large-scale strikes in capital, Trump run/take-over/oil statements, aircraft and air-defense targeting, Rubio denial and Department of War hitting threats while agents entered/exited/on ground, AP no visible U.S. presence.

Argument theory: Preponderance should resolve yes because the proposition does not require nationwide or permanent occupation. Evidence shows U.S. commenced offensive force inside Venezuela to control at least the target compound/operational area long enough to seize and remove Maduro, disable/hit threats, and then assert leverage/control over transition and oil/quarantine conditions. Address defense: law-enforcement label and no visible long-term occupation reduce but do not defeat the 'any portion' control-intent threshold; Rubio's own explanation admits Department of War went in and hit threats while on the ground. Provenance: official admissions are strong for conduct, self-serving for legal labels; ABC and Brookings supply credible-source consensus and adverse context.

Unresolved gaps for later: full RollCall/White House transcript and Reuters/AP direct articles could strengthen consensus. Need later if defendant challenges source chain or argues excerpts are incomplete. Could retry with browser download or alternate mirrors for full PDF binary.

Defendant merits notes, 2026-06-30T00:00Z. Opportunity arguments:defendant. Refreshed get_case/list_evidence: plaintiff has submitted four text evidence items and one technical report; no other case-packet files. Stat/read completed for all four: ev_beef2fca851d... War Department article, 4133 bytes, sha256 beef2f...; ev_90b0943b9140... Lead IG extract, 4373 bytes, sha256 90b094...; ev_51105c5e227a... Brookings analysis, 4797 bytes, sha256 51105...; ev_9a260d070386... ABC transcript, 5379 bytes, sha256 9a260... Evidence assessment: PX-1/PX-2 are strong for U.S. armed operation/capture/extraction in Caracas but do not identify territory held after the operation, occupation force, administrative control, exclusion duration, or orders to establish territorial control. PX-3 is mixed and best for defense: Rubio says not an invasion, did not occupy a country, arrest/law-enforcement operation, FBI agents arrested Maduro, Department of War hit threats while agents entered/exited/on ground, limited targeted trigger-based operation; AP saw no visible U.S. presence/no sign U.S. running Venezuela immediately after. PX-4 is secondary; useful adverse points include no direct governing role, much of Maduro regime remained in power incl. Rodriguez/Padrino, speculation of negotiated nonresistance, and no large-scale ground force assembled to run the country. Decision: no new defense evidence needed now; plaintiff's own record is enough for burden argument, and adding outside material may distract/strengthen plaintiff. Reserve AP/Reuters/White House transcript chain for rebuttal if needed. Theory: concede military force; argue plaintiff has not proved the required intent to establish territorial control over a portion of Venezuela. Tactical protected battlespace or an arrest perimeter is not territorial control; coercive economic/oil/diplomatic leverage is influence, not control of land. Journal updated at /aar-codex/aar-work-notes.md.

Plaintiff rebuttal notes.

Opportunity: `rebuttals:plaintiff`; final actions `submit_rebuttal` or pass; text limit 4000; evidence submission allowed. Refreshed `get_case`, `list_evidence`, and `case_status`. Defendant submitted no new source evidence; it re-offered plaintiff's exhibits as DX-1 through DX-4 and filed a defense technical report.

Defense argument to answer: defense concedes U.S. forces conducted serious armed operation in Caracas, used military aircraft and special capabilities, and removed Maduro and wife. It argues this proves force/intervention but not intent to establish territorial control. It relies on Rubio's law-enforcement/arrest framing, no visible U.S. presence, no large ground force, and no explicit evidence of continuing U.S.-held land.

Rebuttal analysis: strongest response is that defense imposes a higher burden than the proposition. The proposition requires intended control over any portion of Venezuela, not sustained occupation, formal governance, or a large ground force. Rubio's own facts support plaintiff: not friendly territory, Department of War went in, hit threats going in, on way out, and while on the ground. That demonstrates U.S. forces created and maintained a temporary protected operating area/battlespace on Venezuelan sovereign territory to complete the seizure. Control can be temporary and objective-limited under the phrase 'any portion.' Capture of the ruler from a fortified compound cannot be separated from control over the compound/approach corridor/air defense environment. Post-operation run-Venezuela/oil/quarantine language supplies control-intent context. No new evidence needed; use existing exhibits only.

Defendant surrebuttal notes, 2026-06-30T00:03Z. Opportunity surrebuttals:defendant; final action submit_surrebuttal or pass. Refreshed get_case/list_evidence before filing. No new evidence submitted during plaintiff rebuttal; evidence list remains four plaintiff text items with same metadata. Plaintiff rebuttal point: defense allegedly demands continuing occupation/large ground force; plaintiff emphasizes 'any portion' and says temporary military seizure of compound, air-defense environment, and approach/exit corridor suffices. Analysis: respond directly to 'any portion.' Concede any portion can be small/temporary, but the proposition still requires intent to establish control over territory. Plaintiff proves operational means (secured route, suppressed threats, force protection) not a territorial-control objective. Rubio/ABC facts still support targeted arrest/trigger-based operation; AP no visible U.S. presence and Brookings no direct governing role/no large ground force remain limiting. No need for new evidence; plaintiff rebuttal is inference from existing record. Journal updated at /aar-codex/aar-work-notes.md.

Plaintiff closing notes.

Opportunity: `closings:plaintiff`; final action `deliver_closing_statement`; evidence submission and technical reports not allowed; text limit 5000. Refreshed evidence list and stat metadata for all four exhibits. No new evidence or changed metadata found. All four plaintiff evidence items remain juror-visible text/plain with stable hashes.

Closing synthesis: burden is preponderance. Proposition requires a military offensive intended to establish control over any portion of Venezuela, not permanent occupation or nationwide governance. Key proof: PX-1/PX-2 official sources admit U.S. armed forces operation in Caracas, fortified military fortress/compound, months of planning, special operations/joint forces, 150 aircraft, ground extraction force, Maduro removed to USS Iwo Jima/U.S., and U.S. oversight/running transition language. PX-3 ABC transcript corroborates large-scale strikes in capital, warplanes targeting air-defense/military targets, helicopters carrying extraction team, Trump run/take-over/oil statements, Rubio's admission that Department of War went in and hit threats going in/out/on ground, while also preserving Rubio's no-invasion/no-occupation defense and AP no-visible-presence adverse fact. PX-4 Brookings secondary analysis supports credible-source framing and acknowledges adverse points.

Best defense point: no sustained occupation/large ground force/no visible U.S. presence; Rubio called it arrest/no invasion. Closing answer: those facts narrow the form of invasion but do not defeat the actual definition. The United States controlled at least the compound/approach/air-defense/battlespace temporarily to seize Maduro, and did so as part of transition/oil leverage. Official labels are self-serving; official factual admissions are decisive.

Defendant closing notes, 2026-06-30T00:05Z. Opportunity closings:defendant; final action deliver_closing_statement only; evidence submission unavailable. Scanned status/evidence before filing: case active, closing opportunity ready, list_evidence unchanged with four plaintiff-submitted text exhibits only; no new case-packet files or changed evidence metadata. Plaintiff closing reiterates 'any portion,' protected battlespace, and temporary control theory; says labels not controlling and classified orders/maps not required. Closing plan: concede force/intervention/removal; focus on proposition's required intent to establish territorial control. Answer strongest contrary point: 'any portion' can be small/temporary, but must still be a place intended to be controlled as territory, not incidental tactical dominance for an extraction. Record lacks duration, boundary, post-operation control, handoff, U.S. governance, exclusion, command arrangement, or visible presence. Official sources prove conduct but describe capture/extraction. ABC/Rubio/AP and Brookings adverse facts undercut control. Journal updated at /aar-codex/aar-work-notes.md.
